content:
I just lost my .config and had to set up a new one. However, even though make rustavailable was happy, it didn't work. Turns out, the default config had MODVERSIONS and BTF, which disables Rust, and I had to go through and change a bunch of settings.

Can we make this easier?
I've had this problem before. +1
How did you enable Rust? Did you hand edit the config and set CONFIG_RUST=y? In that case I would say the system works as intended.
If you go through menuconfig, you should not be able to enable Rust if prerequisites are wrong.
There is a separate problem where rust is silently disabled when rustavailable target is not happy. That should throw an error instead, but it is a different issue.
I always use menuconfig

Andreas Hindborg said:

If you go through menuconfig, you should not be able to enable Rust if prerequisites are wrong.

The problem is that the default config I ended up with required disabling a bunch of other options before I could enable Rust.
Which .. I could do. But it was a PITA.

Alice Ryhl said:

Which .. I could do. But it was a PITA.

I think that is just kconfig working as intended. One way to go about it is to find the CONFIG_RUST symbol in menuconfig, and look at the prerequisites. Then go enable them one by one.

I think the reason it is like this is because there might not be a unique way to satisfy the dependency graph. So rather than choose _a_ solution, kconfig just does not do that.

One way to go about it is to find the CONFIG_RUST symbol in menuconfig, and look at the prerequisites. Then go enable them one by one.

That's what I did.
I don't know what I did differently, but previously I have not had to do this.
Because those options were not enabled, or not blocking Rust, not sure.

You get this text:

Symbol: RUST [=y]
Type  : bool
Defined at init/Kconfig:1884
  Prompt: Rust support
  Depends on: HAVE_RUST [=y] && RUST_IS_AVAILABLE [=y] && !MODVERSIONS [=n] && !GCC_PLUGINS [=n] && !RANDSTRUCT [=n] && (!DEBUG_INFO_BTF [=n] || PAHOLE_HAS_LANG_EXCLUDE [=n])
  Location:
    -> General setup
      -> Rust support (RUST [=y])
Selects: CONSTRUCTORS [=y]

and then you go to town for anything that is not satisfied.

Alice Ryhl said:

I don't know what I did differently, but previously I have not had to do this.

Maybe your base config changed?
Perhaps?
All I want is a simple button that tells me whether my patchset compiles before I sent it to the list.

Alice Ryhl said:

All I want is a simple button that tells me whether my patchset compiles before I sent it to the list.

I don't have that button, but what I do is this (its a justfile):

config path="linux" +FRAGMENTS="null":
    #!/bin/bash
    set -e
    set -x
    rm -f {{path}}-build/.config
    version="$({{path}}/scripts/min-tool-version.sh bindgen)"
    export PATH="{{top}}/bindgen-$version/bin:$PATH"
    (cd {{path}} && make -j $(nproc) O={{top}}/{{path}}-build LLVM=1 \
      allnoconfig)
      {{path}}/scripts/kconfig/merge_config.sh -m -O {{path}}-build {{path}}-build/.config \
      config/default.fragment \
      `echo "{{FRAGMENTS}}" | sed -E 's#([^[:space:]]+)#config/\1.fragment#g'`
      (cd {{path}} && make -j $(nproc) O={{top}}/{{path}}-build LLVM=1 \
      olddefconfig)

My fragment for enabling rust is:

CONFIG_MODVERSIONS=n
CONFIG_RUST=y

My base config happens to be compatible with enabling Rust except for the modversions thing.

I did consider adding a piece of scripting to check that all the options I enable is actually enabled in the final config, but I did not get around to that yet.

Alice Ryhl said:

All I want is a simple button that tells me whether my patchset compiles before I sent it to the list.

You can use kunit test scripts for that purpose.
Anyway, it's also painful losing your .config ;-)
I lost it from trying to run kunit ...
Ah, I see, I guess it was because kuint used a "O=" build and it wanted you to clean your work directory first, and that part also cleaned your .config.

Turns out, the default config had MODVERSIONS and BTF,

defconfig does not have either enabled by default -- perhaps it was picking your distro one or another base?

so I added this in kernel/configs/rust.config:

diff --git a/kernel/configs/rust.config b/kernel/configs/rust.config
index 2c6e001a7284..c23541841bd8 100644
--- a/kernel/configs/rust.config
+++ b/kernel/configs/rust.config
@@ -1,2 +1,5 @@
 # Help: Enable Rust
+CONFIG_GCC_PLUGINS=n
+CONFIG_MODVERSIONS=n
+CONFIG_DEBUG_INFO_BTF=n
 CONFIG_RUST=y

and make LLVM=1 rust.config will enable Rust in my current .config.
It should not be needed if you are using defconfig and LLVM=1.
make LLVM=1 defconfig rust.config

Miguel Ojeda said:

It should not be needed if you are using defconfig and LLVM=1.

but it would be useful if you just want to enable Rust in some config you already have?
For us, yeah, but it may do something unexpected for others, i.e. it seems safer to not disable things.
And if we are OK disabling things, we could also disable IBT and RETHUNK for the time being, for instance.
That would be convenient for us, but maybe a surprise for others.

Having a file (and in particular the "default" file named rust.config) that essentially "forces" Rust to be enabled disregarding other options people may already have in their config sounds a bit risky. If they use defconfig, it is an explicit step to use that as a base. If they use menuconfig to disable stuff, it is also an explicit step.

Alice Ryhl said:

I lost it from trying to run kunit ...

git worktree is your friend ;-)
Ah, I am already using that for other things, but I didn't think of using it for this.
I have a dedicated linux-kunit work directory, and only do git reset --hard and run kunit in that directory.

BTW, if you have a kernel.org account, or you ask explicitly, the 0day build testing bot will watch your git repo, and try to run a few build tests along with other tests when you create or update a branch. It provides pretty good build coverage.
Ah, that could be a reason to get an account.

Boqun Feng said:

BTW, if you have a kernel.org account, or you ask explicitly, the 0day build testing bot will watch your git repo, and try to run a few build tests along with other tests when you create or update a branch. It provides pretty good build coverage.

Somehow my GitHub fork is watched (and I didn't ask for that) and it's sometimes annoying when I was doing dev work and got an email from kernel test robot yelling me that my WIP branch doesn't work :(

Gary Guo said:

Boqun Feng said:

BTW, if you have a kernel.org account, or you ask explicitly, the 0day build testing bot will watch your git repo, and try to run a few build tests along with other tests when you create or update a branch. It provides pretty good build coverage.

Somehow my GitHub fork is watched (and I didn't ask for that) and it's sometimes annoying when I was doing dev work and got an email from kernel test robot yelling me that my WIP branch doesn't work :(

You can reply and ask them to not watch your repo (or only watch some branches)
